does: This paper characterizes and systematically evaluates Trojan Hippo attacks, a class of persistent memory attacks against LLM agents where attackers embed dormant payloads into an agent's long-term memory through a single untrusted tool interaction such as a crafted email. The payload remains inert until the user discusses sensitive topics like finance, health, or identity, at which point it activates to exfiltrate high-value personal data. The authors introduce a dynamic evaluation framework with two components: an OpenEvolve-based adaptive red-teaming benchmark that continuously refines attacks to stress-test memory defenses and backends, and a capability-aware security-utility analysis specifically designed for persistent memory systems to enable principled assessment of defense tradeoffs.
connects: This work extends prior research on memory poisoning attacks and LLM agent security by examining attacks in a more realistic threat model where attackers gain access through ordinary user interactions rather than direct system compromise. It builds on foundational work on adversarial prompts and prompt injection attacks, but focuses specifically on the persistence dimension enabled by agent memory architectures. The paper responds to emerging concerns about memory systems in deployed agentic systems by systematizing what were previously only anecdotal demonstrations. It connects to broader work on evaluating AI safety mechanisms and security properties of complex AI systems, bringing systematic benchmarking methodology from the adversarial machine learning literature to the agent memory domain.
enables: This work enables future research into robust memory architectures and filtering mechanisms that can preserve agent utility while mitigating exfiltration risks. It points toward the development of memory-aware threat models and formal security guarantees for persistent agent systems, which are increasingly critical as LLM agents handle sensitive user information across extended interactions. The capability-aware security-utility framework provides tools for evaluating proposed defenses in a principled manner, potentially spurring innovation in memory segregation, encryption, and access control mechanisms. The systematic evaluation methodology and benchmarks could catalyze industry adoption of memory safety practices and inform the design of next-generation agent architectures that balance personalization with security.
